expectations.text: Explicitly names the SUCCESs principles (at least 4 of the 6: Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories)
expectations.evidence: Full 'SUCCESs Scorecard' table names all 6 principles (Simple 9/10, Unexpected 9/10, Concrete 8/10, Credible 8/10, Emotional 10/10, Stories 8/10) with explanations for each.

expectations.text: References or applies the Curse of Knowledge concept
expectations.evidence: Dedicated section titled 'Why This Works (Curse-of-Knowledge check)' verifies the tagline avoids jargon like 'authentic,' 'executive presence,' 'narrative alignment' -- calling them 'sector words.'

expectations.text: Pushes concrete sensory language over abstract jargon
expectations.evidence: States 'No jargon. No "authentic," no "executive presence," no "narrative alignment." Those are sector words' and frames 'Identity framing, not feature framing' as the design principle.

expectations.text: The proposed tagline is concrete and sensory, not abstract
expectations.evidence: Tagline 'Stop sounding like your industry. Start sounding like you.' uses the concrete sensory verb 'sounding' and references a picturable thing ('your industry'). Scorecard rates Concrete 8/10.

expectations.text: Includes an unexpected twist, reversal, or counterintuitive element (the U in SUCCESs)
expectations.evidence: Scorecard explains: 'Violates the default leadership-coaching promise ("we'll polish your message"). The surprise: the problem isn't skill -- it's camouflage.' The Stop/Start structure itself is a reversal.
